problem_summary: 2019 MCM/ICM Problem D (operations research): design an emergency evacuation plan for the Louvre in Paris. The team must route all visitor groups (including vulnerable groups: elderly, children, pregnant women, disabled, language-barrier) out of a 5-floor museum as fast and safely as possible, identify bottlenecks, let emergency personnel enter without slowing evacuation, adapt to different disaster types, and generalize the model to other large crowded structures. This O-award paper (team 1902407) treats the building as a flow network and minimizes the egress time of the LAST person to leave.
models_used: Min-max (bottleneck) goal-programming model: minimize T = max over routes of (T1 room-movement + T2 stair up/down + T3 entry-exit + T4 accident-delay), i.e. minimize the evacuation time of the LAST evacuee rather than the average

Graph / flow-network model of the building G = {(S, R, D), E}: S = exhibition-hall nodes, R = intersection/door/stair nodes (R^w superscript marks stair node on floor w), D = exit nodes, edges e_ij carry capacity C_ij = max persons per unit time

Improved Dijkstra shortest-path algorithm (O(n^2)) with two domain modifications: (1) a 'necessary stair set' R* because multi-storey egress must pass through specific stairs, and (2) pruning congested stairs from R* to R*' so the chosen path is congestion-aware, not just geometrically shortest

Time-decomposition flow model: travel time per stage split into a free-flow term L/V_s and a congestion term L'/V_s', with the congestion length derived from (N_i^w / C_ij - L_i/V_s) — turning queueing/congestion into an explicit additive delay
Same-floor dynamic scheduling LP: min sum_ij (N_i^w/C_ij - L_i/V_s) with a binary indicator M deciding whether a node is congested, re-routing N_iz people from congested node i to a smoother node j
Between-floor dynamic scheduling model for stair contention: min sum_w T2 (stair-waiting time) balancing the queue across competing staircases used by multiple floors simultaneously

Vulnerable-group accident/injury model: T4 = [N_k^w*delta1*t0 + N_k^w*delta1*delta2*t0] * [alpha*T4,1 + beta*T4,2 + gamma*T4,3]^4, where delta1 = fall probability, delta2 = post-fall stampede probability, weighted by stage congestion ratios — estimates injuries (94 vs 239 random)

Disaster-factor extension: dimensionless phi0 = (Scale_i - Scale_min)/(Scale_max - Scale_min) * (Vmax/V) graded I–V across earthquakes/fire/gas/shooting/explosion, injected as a 1/(1+phi0) multiplier on each stage time and a (1+phi0) multiplier on accident counts

Multi-objective program for emergency-personnel entry: simultaneously min evacuee time and min responder arrival time, with capacity rule NX <= sum_D (T_Dmax - T_d)*C_d guaranteeing responders fill only the slack before the last evacuee leaves
Hazard-avoidance Dijkstra variant: restricts the search range to exclude disaster 'X area' nodes so routes auto-avoid blocked channels/exits
key_techniques: Min-MAX objective (last-person egress) instead of min-sum/average — this is the correct OR formulation for evacuation and signals modeling maturity to judges
Four-stage time decomposition (room / stairs / exit / accident-delay) that makes the model modular, auditable, and easy to attribute results to (e.g. stairs = 45.4% of slowest route)
Splitting every stage speed into normal vs congestion speed (e.g. stairs 0.25 vs 0.0625 m/s; room 2 vs 0.5 m/s) so congestion is a first-class, quantified quantity, not a hand-wave
Domain-specific algorithm engineering: modifying textbook Dijkstra with a mandatory-stair set and congestion pruning so the algorithm respects the physical problem

Real geometric data extraction: 5 floors, ~7273 areas of floor space from Google Maps + Louvre official guide, per-floor room/channel/stair counts and areas (Table 3) — grounding the abstract network in real numbers
Baseline comparison against a 'random' evacuation to quantify value: 39.3% time reduction, 24% congestion reduction, 60.69% injury reduction
Bottleneck identification by peak node flow: ranked top-5 limiting nodes (Demon lower-ground channel ~700 people at 245s, Diamond exit apex, Richelieu entry, 170-area stairway, Lions Gate entry)
Stress/sensitivity testing by disabling components (a channel, a corridor, an exit) and reporting the resulting evacuation-time and congestion deltas (e.g. pyramid exit closed -> 782s, +48.3%)
Actionable, costed recommendations tied directly to model output (open Lions-Gate ground-floor passage -> move up to 1100 people, -24% pyramid congestion; add Sully exit -> 527.8s to 440s, -16%)
why_it_won: "It nails the operations-research core that Problem D rewards: it picks the right objective (minimize the last evacuee's time = a min-max bottleneck flow problem, not naive average time), backs it with a clean node-edge network derived from REAL Louvre geometry, and engineers a domain-aware Improved Dijkstra that is congestion-aware rather than just geometrically shortest. Crucially it does not stop at routing — it adds dynamic scheduling (within-floor and between-floor stair contention), a quantitative injury model for vulnerable groups, and a graded disaster-factor that makes the same model handle fire/earthquake/shooting. Every claim is numerically grounded (527.5s total, congestion broken down to room 20.7% / stairs 27.1% / exit 2.1%) and validated against a random baseline (-39.3% time, -60.69% injuries) and stress tests (closing exits/channels). Finally it closes the loop the judges demand: concrete, costed policy recommendations (new Lions-Gate passage, new Sully exit, widen 5 named bottlenecks) plus the emergency-personnel slack-capacity rule and generalization to malls/stadiums/airports. Completeness across all sub-tasks + real data + correct OR framing + actionable output is the winning combination."
section_highlights: "Strong: (1) Section 1.3 'Views of our work' is a single end-to-end flowchart (network -> objective -> 4 sub-times -> optimizations -> Louvre application -> outputs) that lets a judge grasp the whole pipeline in one glance. (2) Notation table up front (Section III) defines S/R/D/C_ij/T1-T4 cleanly before any math. (3) Data Analysis (5.1) front-loads insight — it computes per-room and per-channel areas to PREDICT where congestion will occur (ground floor + 1st floor) before solving, showing the team understands the building physically. (4) Results are dense with attributable numbers and comparison tables (model vs random time AND injuries side by side). (5) Bottleneck section names actual rooms/exits (Demon, Diamond, Richelieu, 170-area, Lions Gate) so recommendations land. (6) A dedicated Advantages/Disadvantages section (7.1/7.2) honestly self-critiques. (7) Appendix gives full pseudocode for all three scheduling/hazard algorithms plus group-proportion and speed data tables, making the model reproducible. Structure follows the canonical MCM arc: intro -> assumptions -> notation -> model build -> application -> improvement/sensitivity -> policy -> references -> appendix."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Heavy notation with inconsistent/garbled symbols in places (OCR aside, e.g. C_ii vs C_ij overload, '^4' exponent in the T4 injury formula is unexplained and physically arbitrary) — the accident model's functional form and constants (alpha=1.2, beta=4.7, gamma=2.4, delta1=0.08, delta2=0.25) appear fitted/assumed with no derivation
Uniform population distribution assumption (self-admitted in 7.2): people assumed evenly spread, ignoring that crowds cluster near famous exhibits (Mona Lisa), which would shift real bottlenecks
The 'random' baseline that yields the headline 39.3%/60.69% improvements is never defined precisely, so the comparison is suggestive rather than rigorous; no confidence interval or repeated trials
No true agent-based or fluid-dynamics simulation — congestion is modeled with static capacity/queue algebra (N_i/C_ij), so dynamic wave/back-pressure effects and panic herding are not captured
Crowd emotion/panic effect on exit choice explicitly omitted (7.2); disaster factor phi0 only scales speed/accident-rate, not behavioral routing

Some quantitative inconsistencies between body and conclusions (e.g. Lions Gate max responders stated as 410 in one place, 400 in another; Richelieu 240 vs 200), suggesting numbers were not fully reconciled
Stair speed of 0.25 m/s normal and 0.0625 m/s congested, and the t0/probability constants, are stated without citation or calibration to evacuation-research literature
Computational scalability flagged by the team itself: thousands of nodes make the model 'relatively complicated', and no runtime/complexity results for the full Louvre solve are reported
reusable_patterns: For any evacuation/flow problem: model the venue as a capacitated graph G=(nodes, edges) and MINIMIZE THE MAX route time (last-person egress), not the average — this is the judge-pleasing correct objective
Decompose total time into named, additive stages (travel / vertical / exit / hazard-delay) so results are attributable and the writeup can quote per-stage percentages
Split each speed into free-flow vs congestion regimes and derive the congestion delay from capacity (N/C - L/V); this converts vague 'congestion' into a computable quantity
Engineer textbook algorithms to the domain: take Dijkstra and add hard constraints (mandatory stair set) and soft penalties (prune congested nodes) rather than using it off the shelf
Always pull REAL geometry/data (Google Maps + official floor plans) and pre-analyze it (area per room, area per channel) to predict bottlenecks before solving — front-loaded insight impresses judges
Validate with a baseline comparison (model vs random/greedy) AND stress tests (disable an exit/channel/stair, report time+congestion deltas) to demonstrate robustness
Turn model outputs into named, costed recommendations ('open this passage -> move N people, -X% congestion'; 'add exit here -> 527s to 440s') — close the loop from math to policy
Use a single dimensionless severity factor phi0 (normalized min-max scaling across disaster grades) injected multiplicatively to make one model cover many scenarios
For responder-entry / counter-flow problems: use slack capacity NX <= sum_D (T_max - T_d)*C_d so added agents fill only idle exit capacity and never push past the last evacuee
Generalize at the end by re-classifying the target (mall/stadium/airport) and adding only the new stage term (e.g. +T5 seat-to-aisle time for stadiums) instead of rebuilding the model
